user_goals_summary: Documentation should build the reader's mental model of why the system was designed this way, not just describe what it does. Prose carries conceptual weight so code examples stay short and scannable. The reader should finish knowing enough to evaluate future changes, not just follow current instructions.

rubric.name: Design Decision Reasoning
rubric.description: Every documented design choice names the rejected alternative explicitly and explains why it was worse, using the 'we chose X over Y because Z' framing. The reasoning gives the reader enough context to evaluate proposed changes, not just understand current behavior — this is what they can't get from reading the code.

dimensions.label: Each design choice names at least one specific rejected alternative
Tradeoffs of the chosen approach are stated explicitly, not just its benefits
Explanations convey architectural reasoning that cannot be derived from reading the implementation
The reasoning gives the reader enough context to evaluate whether a future change would be appropriate

rubric.name: Prose-Code Weight Distribution
rubric.description: Explanatory prose before code examples front-loads the conceptual model, structural choices, and failure modes so the code only needs to show the shape of the pattern. If something is explained by an inline comment, the prose hasn't done its job yet.

dimensions.label: Why the code is shaped a particular way (e.g., mutex strategy, helper functions, branching) is explained in prose before the code block
Every notable construct in the code (helpers, naming distinctions, two-phase patterns) is introduced in the preceding prose
Warnings, preconditions, and concurrency requirements appear before the code block they apply to, not after
Inline code comments do not restate what the surrounding prose has already explained

rubric.name: Code Example Discipline
rubric.description: Each code example has a one-line heading comment stating what it accomplishes (not how), stays at or under ~15 lines so the reader can hold the whole function in their head at once, and avoids carrying conceptual weight that belongs in prose.

dimensions.label: Every code example has a single-line comment at the top describing what it accomplishes
Functions and code blocks are approximately 15 lines or fewer
The heading comment lets the reader determine whether this snippet is relevant before reading any code
When a code block is long, the fix is moving explanation to prose rather than cutting logic

rubric.name: Recommendation Framing
rubric.description: When recommending an approach, the doc names the alternative pattern, acknowledges that it works, and then explains concretely why it's the worse default. Directives become reasoning the reader internalizes rather than instructions they follow blindly.

dimensions.label: The recommended approach is compared to at least one named alternative pattern
The alternative is acknowledged as functional, not dismissed outright
The specific weakness of the alternative is explained concretely
The reader finishes knowing why to use the recommendation, not just that they should

rubric.name: Narrative Coherence
rubric.description: Section headers and reading order tell a coherent story from setup through integration through troubleshooting. Later sections reference earlier design decisions via callbacks rather than re-explanation, and cross-section dependencies are named explicitly.

dimensions.label: Section headers convey a narrative progression rather than just categorizing contents
Later sections reference earlier design reasoning with explicit callbacks rather than re-explaining
When a concept depends on another section, the dependency is named with a direct reference
The document reads as a coherent whole from start to finish, not as independently authored sections

rubric.name: Practical Reference Value
rubric.description: The doc includes structured reference material — error tables, FAQs, summary comparisons — that engineers will search for during incidents or integration. Answers address the specific scenario the engineer is in and tell them what to do, not just what went wrong.

dimensions.label: Error codes, status codes, and common problems are in structured formats (tables, labeled Q&A)
FAQ and error entries address scenarios engineers encounter under pressure (debugging at 2am, unexpected errors in production)
Each error or FAQ entry includes what to do next, not just an explanation of what happened
Key properties and comparisons are summarized in tables for quick scanning

coaching_notes: This user's core belief is that documentation should transmit architectural reasoning — 'why this way and not another way' — because that's the only thing engineers can't get from reading the code. They will push back repeatedly until rejected alternatives are named and tradeoffs are explicit. They have a strong sense of prose-code division of labor: prose carries conceptual weight, code shows pattern shape, and the ~15-line ceiling is a hard signal that prose hasn't pulled enough weight. They give very precise, targeted feedback and explicitly approve ('keep verbatim') when satisfied, so track what they've locked in and don't revisit it.

dimensions.label: Section headers convey a narrative progression rather than just categorizing contents
Later sections reference earlier design reasoning with explicit callbacks rather than re-explaining
When a concept depends on another section, the dependency is named with a direct reference

coaching_notes: This user's strongest instinct is that documentation should teach architectural intuition, not just describe implementation. They consistently push for naming rejected alternatives and explaining tradeoffs. Their second strongest instinct is code economy — examples should be scannable at a glance, with prose doing the conceptual heavy lifting beforehand. When they say something is 'too long', the fix is almost always better prose above the code, not less code.
refinement_summary: Removed the hallucinated 'Practical Reference Value' criterion. Reordered priorities to match the user's explicit importance ranking (Code Example Discipline moved to #2 above Prose-Code Weight Distribution). Added a 'precise_language' dimension to Narrative Coherence to capture the user's preference for respecting reader competence.
_change_explanation:
- **Removed "Practical Reference Value"**: The user classified this criterion as hallucinated. While the doc happened to include error tables and FAQs, the user does not consider structured reference material a standalone writing preference — it was an artifact of this particular task, not a transferable value.
- **Reordered priorities**: The user's importance ranking placed Code Example Discipline (#2) above Prose-Code Weight Distribution (#3), which was the opposite of the original rubric. Updated to match the user's explicit ranking.
- **Added "precise_language" dimension to Narrative Coherence**: The user's cold-start preferences emphasize writing for engineers who are "smart but haven't touched this particular system before." This, combined with the conversation's consistent avoidance of minimizing language, warranted a dimension capturing the preference for precise, non-condescending language. It fits naturally in Narrative Coherence alongside the existing tone-adjacent dimensions about progression and callbacks.

rubric.name: Narrative Coherence
rubric.description: Section headers and reading order tell a coherent story — the doc flows like a guided walkthrough even when it's reference material. Headers describe what is happening at each stage, not just label the mechanism. When two sections are conceptually entangled, the one with more dangerous failure modes or that serves as a prerequisite for correct reasoning comes first.

dimensions.label: Section headers describe what is happening at each stage rather than labeling the mechanism — e.g., 'Checking Whether the Cached Token Is Still Usable' rather than 'Expiry Check'

coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: rejected alternatives must be named for every design decision; every code block gets a heading comment; warnings appear before the code they apply to. Preferred tradeoff: when a code example is too long or requires too much scaffolding, narrow the slice and move explanation to prose rather than adding context to the code. Responds to feedback by immediately identifying the precise gap between prose and code — typically the prose isn't pulling enough weight, and the fix is always structural (front-load explanation) rather than cosmetic (add comments).

rubric.name: Design Decision Reasoning
rubric.description: Every documented design choice names the rejected alternative explicitly and explains why it was worse, using the 'we chose X over Y because Z' framing. The rejection reasoning must name the concrete mechanism, property, or coupling that made the alternative worse — not just that it was worse or that it introduced vague problems. The reasoning gives the reader enough context to evaluate proposed changes, not just understand current behavior — this is what they can't get from reading the code.

dimensions.label: Each design choice names at least one specific rejected alternative
Tradeoffs of the chosen approach are stated explicitly, not just its benefits
Explanations convey architectural reasoning that cannot be derived from reading the implementation
The reasoning gives the reader enough context to evaluate whether a future change would be appropriate
The rejection reasoning names the specific mechanism, coupling, or property that made the alternative worse — not just that it introduced problems or race conditions

rubric.name: Code Example Discipline
rubric.description: Each code example has a one-line heading comment stating what it accomplishes (not how), stays at or under ~15 lines so the reader can hold the whole function in their head at once, and is self-contained enough to be intelligible without mentally gap-filling undefined references. When an example requires too much scaffolding to be intelligible, the fix is narrowing the slice to show only the caller-facing pattern, not adding more context. When the point being made is an ordering constraint or behavioral invariant, the code example should demonstrate it directly — not just assert it in prose.

dimensions.label: Every code example has a single-line comment at the top describing what it accomplishes
The heading comment lets the reader determine whether this snippet is relevant before reading any code
Functions and code blocks are approximately 15 lines or fewer
The example does not reference undefined external functions, types, or helpers that force the reader to mentally gap-fill; when too much scaffolding would be needed, the slice is narrowed instead
When a code block is long, the fix is moving explanation to prose rather than cutting logic
When the point being made is an ordering constraint or behavioral invariant (e.g., tombstone checked before TTL), the code demonstrates it directly rather than relying solely on prose assertion

rubric.name: Prose-Code Weight Distribution
rubric.description: Explanatory prose before code examples front-loads the conceptual model, structural choices, and failure modes so the code only needs to show the shape of the pattern. If something is explained by an inline comment, the prose hasn't done its job yet. Warnings and preconditions appear before the section where someone would encounter the problem, not after. When the code's structure itself is the argument — for example, the ordering of checks encodes a security invariant — prose should frame the question the code answers, not pre-explain the answer the reader should see in the code.

dimensions.label: Why the code is shaped a particular way (e.g., mutex strategy, helper functions, branching) is explained in prose before the code block
Every notable construct in the code (helpers, naming distinctions, two-phase patterns) is introduced in the preceding prose
Warnings, preconditions, and concurrency requirements appear before the code block they apply to, not after
Inline code comments do not restate what the surrounding prose has already explained

When code structure carries the argument (e.g., check ordering), prose sets up the question the code answers rather than pre-explaining the answer — letting the code teach rather than merely illustrate

rubric.name: Narrative Coherence
rubric.description: Section headers and reading order tell a coherent story — the doc flows like a guided walkthrough even when it's reference material. Headers describe what is happening at each stage, not just label the mechanism. When two sections are conceptually entangled, the one with more dangerous failure modes or that serves as a prerequisite for correct reasoning comes first.

dimensions.label: Section headers describe what is happening at each stage rather than labeling the mechanism — e.g., 'Checking Whether the Cached Token Is Still Usable' rather than 'Expiry Check'
Content that walks through a sequence of stages is broken into distinct subsections with headers, not presented as a single long block
When two sections are entangled, the one the reader needs to reason correctly about subsequent material comes first — especially state information, pitfalls, or caveats that affect interpretation
Later sections reference earlier design reasoning with explicit callbacks rather than re-explaining
When a concept depends on another section, the dependency is named with a direct reference

rubric.name: Recommendation Framing
rubric.description: When recommending an approach, the doc names the alternative pattern, acknowledges that it works, and then explains concretely why it's the worse default. Directives become reasoning the reader internalizes rather than instructions they follow blindly.

dimensions.label: The recommended approach is compared to at least one named alternative pattern
The alternative is acknowledged as functional, not dismissed outright
The specific weakness of the alternative is explained concretely
The reader finishes knowing why to use the recommendation, not just that they should

user_goals_summary: Documentation should build the reader's mental model of why the system works this way, not just describe what it does. Every design choice names the rejected alternative and its cost. Code examples are concise, self-contained, and front-loaded with a heading comment so the reader knows whether a snippet is relevant before reading it.
coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: rejected alternatives must be named for every design decision; every code block gets a heading comment; warnings appear before the code they apply to. Preferred tradeoff: when a code example is too long or requires too much scaffolding, narrow the slice and move explanation to prose rather than adding context to the code. Responds to feedback by immediately identifying the precise gap between prose and code — typically the prose isn't pulling enough weight, and the fix is always structural (front-load explanation) rather than cosmetic (add comments).

user_goals_summary: Documentation should build durable architectural intuition by explaining why the system is built the way it is — naming rejected alternatives and their specific costs — so that engineers new to the system can reason about future changes, not just follow current procedures. Code examples should be tight, self-contained, and scaffolded by prose that front-loads every structural choice, warning, and conceptual model the reader needs before they encounter the code.

rubric.name: Design Decision Reasoning
rubric.description: Every documented design choice names the rejected alternative explicitly and explains why it was worse, using the 'we chose X over Y because Z' framing. The rejection reasoning must name the concrete mechanism, property, or coupling that made the alternative worse — not just that it was worse or that it introduced vague problems. The reasoning gives the reader enough context to evaluate proposed changes, not just understand current behavior — this is what they can't get from reading the code.

dimensions.label: Each design choice names at least one specific rejected alternative
Tradeoffs of the chosen approach are stated explicitly, not just its benefits
Explanations convey architectural reasoning that cannot be derived from reading the implementation
The reasoning gives the reader enough context to evaluate whether a future change would be appropriate
The rejection reasoning names the specific mechanism, coupling, or property that made the alternative worse — not just that it introduced problems

When an example requires too much scaffolding to be intelligible, the fix is narrowing the slice to show only the caller-facing pattern, not adding more context. When the point being made is an ordering constraint or behavioral invariant, the code example should demonstrate it directly.

coaching_notes: This user's non-negotiable is the 'chose X over Y because Z' pattern — any explanation of system behavior that doesn't name a rejected alternative reads as 'the code commenting on itself.' They treat warning/pitfall placement as a safety issue: if a dangerous caveat appears after the reader has formed a mental model, it may as well not exist. Their preferred trade-off is always: make prose do more work so code can be tighter — never the reverse.
